caveats: a: The frozen labor-share input is missing, so the scale of labor opportunity cannot be anchored to industry compensation and receipts.
a: Blueberry evidence may not represent cranberries, raspberries, blackberries, currants, and other berries with different harvest systems.
a: Mechanization is not automatically AI exposure, and some machine-harvest tasks are already automated.
rho: USDA examples span specialty crops and include prototypes rather than only commercially durable berry deployments.
rho: Implementation varies sharply between fresh and processing markets and by farm layout.
rho: Mechanical aids may raise worker productivity without eliminating accountable labor.
e: The frozen LMM firm count is missing, so no eligible-firm count can be derived from this share.
e: The contract-incidence proxy is for all U.S. farms rather than berry farms or the LMM band.
e: Some economically service-like growing arrangements may be reported as marketing contracts, creating classification error.
s5: National farm demographics may not represent commercial berry farms or production contractors.
s5: Producer age is not owner age when farms have several decision-makers or entities.
s5: No observed denominator of eligible berry farms and qualifying transfers was found.
q: Contract terms are private and no berry-specific retention sample was found.
q: The eligible lens is unusually narrow, so ordinary farm-gate pricing evidence is only directional.
q: Import competition affects commodity prices and contractor bargaining but is not a direct contractual pass-through measure.
d5: Blueberries are only one component of NAICS 111334.
d5: Production volume and consumer availability are not constant-price demand for the narrowed production service.
d5: Imports can satisfy U.S. berry consumption without creating demand for U.S. farm operators.
o: This is a bounded judgment rather than a directly observed operator-required share.
o: Contractors may vertically integrate and self-operate farms, eliminating external service demand rather than operator accountability.
o: The estimate applies only after the separate demand-ratio adjustment.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring or repeat production-service fees paid by processors, marketers, or other contractors for growing specified berries under contractor production requirements; ordinary farm-gate commodity sales are outside the fixed service lens.
lens.excluded_activities: Farms selling grower-owned berries through spot sales or marketing contracts, hobby and lifestyle farms, captive units, custom farm-support contractors classified outside NAICS 111334, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market berry farms operating under recurring production contracts in which an external contractor owns or directs the commodity and compensates the grower for cultivation services.

a.bridge: USDA evidence describes labor use and automation in blueberries and broader specialty crops, not a wage-weighted task inventory for production-contract berry farms. The range maps exposed scouting, yield estimation, irrigation, spraying, sorting, recordkeeping, scheduling, and machine-enabled harvesting tasks to the narrowed lens while excluding work already mechanized. Broader specialty-crop examples may bias exposure upward, while omission of farm-management and contractor-reporting work may bias it downward.
caveats: The frozen labor-share input is missing, so the scale of labor opportunity cannot be anchored to industry compensation and receipts.
Blueberry evidence may not represent cranberries, raspberries, blackberries, currants, and other berries with different harvest systems.
Mechanization is not automatically AI exposure, and some machine-harvest tasks are already automated.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time-and-task study of contract blueberry, cranberry, raspberry, and blackberry farms, identifying current mechanization and software adoption by task, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: AI-enabled sensing, imaging, decision support, and autonomous equipment can reduce scouting, input-management, harvest-planning, sorting, documentation, and some picking labor. Hand harvesting, pruning, trellis and plant work, equipment repair, food-safety execution, and irregular field handling remain physical; blueberry machine harvesting is established but can reduce fresh-market quality, so current not-yet-automated exposure remains meaningful but bounded.

d5.bridge: USDA measures blueberry output, market destination, consumer availability, and imports rather than five-year constant-price demand for production-contract services across all non-strawberry berries. Record 2024 cultivated-blueberry production and long-run demand growth support expansion, while rising import share and grower price pressure constrain demand for U.S. growing services. Blueberry concentration may bias the estimate if other berry crops follow different trends.
caveats: Blueberries are only one component of NAICS 111334.
Production volume and consumer availability are not constant-price demand for the narrowed production service.
Imports can satisfy U.S. berry consumption without creating demand for U.S. farm operators.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year buyer procurement plans and contracted domestic acreage by berry, joined to import volumes, weather-normalized yields, real grower prices, and production-contract service fees, would materially move the ratio.
d5.rationale: Consumer interest and year-round berry availability support underlying demand, and cultivated blueberry production reached a record in 2024. The domestic service basket faces material substitution from imports, crop-specific weather, water constraints, and buyer sourcing shifts, so growth in U.S. contract-growing demand is likely slower and more uncertain than consumption growth.

e.bridge: USDA reports that production contracts were used by 2% of all U.S. farms in 2020 and that crop farmers predominantly use marketing contracts, while the source population includes all commodities and farm sizes. The range restricts this incidence to berry farms in the specified EBITDA band that render cultivation services under true production contracts and excludes marketing contracts, which leave commodity ownership with the farmer. Because production contracting is concentrated in livestock, the all-farm rate likely biases berry eligibility upward.
caveats: The frozen LMM firm count is missing, so no eligible-firm count can be derived from this share.
The contract-incidence proxy is for all U.S. farms rather than berry farms or the LMM band.
Some economically service-like growing arrangements may be reported as marketing contracts, creating classification error.
e.change_evidence: Commodity-level ARMS microdata for berry farms, separating production from marketing contracts and matched to normalized EBITDA and customer concentration, would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: Census defines the industry as establishments growing berries to the farm gate, which is ordinarily product production rather than outsourced service. Only the uncommon production-contract structure fits the fixed lens; marketing contracts set an outlet or price for grower-owned crops but do not convert the farm into a service provider.

l.detail: SUSB scope excludes this activity, no IPS labor-compensation/output series at any level, and no other receipts source downloaded; labor_share left null (recorded gap).
l.rationale: dataset labor_share has no defensible value

n.detail: margin 0.0804 (margins.json[11]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Farming/Agriculture EBITDA/Sales 8.04%); revenue/employee $299,458 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 11); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: This is a bounded judgment rather than a directly observed operator-required share.
Contractors may vertically integrate and self-operate farms, eliminating external service demand rather than operator accountability.
The estimate applies only after the separate demand-ratio adjustment.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of fully autonomous commercial berry farms operating through repeated seasons without an accountable farm management organization, or buyer self-operation replacing contract growers at scale, would materially lower the estimate.
o.rationale: Nearly all surviving demand for contracted berry cultivation still requires an accountable operator to control land, plants, irrigation, inputs, food safety, labor, harvest timing, equipment, and delivery against buyer specifications. Software and robots can execute tasks but do not eliminate operational accountability for biological production and contract performance.

q.bridge: USDA describes general agricultural production contracts, under which contractors tend to control production terms and compensate growers for services, and blueberry market evidence shows import-driven pressure on grower prices. Neither measures retained AI benefit for contract berry growers. The range treats contract renewal, fee negotiation, quality adjustments, buyer power, and competitive supply as partial pass-through mechanisms; general contract evidence may understate or overstate berry-specific bargaining power.
caveats: Contract terms are private and no berry-specific retention sample was found.
The eligible lens is unusually narrow, so ordinary farm-gate pricing evidence is only directional.
Import competition affects commodity prices and contractor bargaining but is not a direct contractual pass-through measure.
q.change_evidence: A representative sample of berry production contracts showing fee formulas, cost pass-through, capital ownership, productivity adjustments, quality deductions, renewal timing, and renegotiated terms after automation would materially change retention.
q.rationale: An operator can initially retain savings when service fees are fixed and operating costs fall, but concentrated buyers, transparent yields, prescribed practices, quality deductions, and contract renewal can transfer benefits to the contractor. Equipment ownership and financing determine whether the grower or buyer captures automation economics.

rho.bridge: USDA documents specialty-crop automation development and blueberry mechanization constraints, but does not measure five-year implementation of exposed AI labor opportunity on eligible contract berry farms. The range discounts technical applicability for equipment cost, crop and cultivar variation, field architecture, small-farm capital, data availability, harvest quality, food safety, and workforce change. Research-program demonstrations may bias implementation upward relative to commercial adoption.
caveats: USDA examples span specialty crops and include prototypes rather than only commercially durable berry deployments.
Implementation varies sharply between fresh and processing markets and by farm layout.
Mechanical aids may raise worker productivity without eliminating accountable labor.
rho.change_evidence: A panel of eligible farms reporting deployed systems, acreage and task coverage, capital cost, utilization, packout, crop damage, labor hours, and contractor acceptance over five harvests would materially move the estimate.
rho.rationale: Decision support, sensing, recordkeeping, and targeted input applications can deploy sooner than selective robotic harvest. Berry harvest automation is technically more mature for processing fruit, but fresh-market packout losses and crop damage constrain substitution; contract specifications and buyer acceptance add another implementation gate.

s5.bridge: The 2022 Census of Agriculture measures producer age and farm demographics for all U.S. farms, not qualifying control transfers among eligible production-contract berry farms. The range translates an average producer age of 58.1 and a 38% share age 65 or older into a five-year transfer probability, then discounts for multiple-producer farms, family continuity, contract nonassignability, and closures without transferable operations. The aging proxy may bias qualifying transfers upward if older producers add family decision-makers without transferring control.
caveats: National farm demographics may not represent commercial berry farms or production contractors.
Producer age is not owner age when farms have several decision-makers or entities.
No observed denominator of eligible berry farms and qualifying transfers was found.
s5.change_evidence: A berry-farm owner-age and succession survey linked to five years of deed, entity-control, production-contract assignment, closure, and M&A outcomes would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: An aging producer population supports material succession pressure, but a producer's retirement does not always create a qualifying control transaction. Family succession, land leasing, contract termination, and farm closure can occur without transferring a viable operating company and customer relationship.

principal_driver: Specialty-crop sensing, decision support, and machine-enabled harvesting expose meaningful berry-farm tasks to automation.
principal_weakness: Very few berry farms appear to meet the fixed outsourced-service lens, and the eligible population cannot be anchored to a frozen LMM count.

sources.supports: Defines the industry as establishments primarily engaged in growing berries and explains that crop production generally ends at the farm gate for first sale or price determination.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS 111334: Berry (except Strawberry) Farming

sources.supports: USDA ERS documents blueberry hand and machine harvesting, harvester costs, fresh-market packout losses, rising mechanization, import competition, and an expected cost-price squeeze; it also reports fruit and tree-nut labor costs reaching 38.5% of production expenses.
sources.title: Supplement to Adjusting to Higher Labor Costs in Selected U.S. Fresh Fruit and Vegetable Industries: Case Studies

sources.supports: USDA NIFA describes commercializing specialty-crop sensors, imaging, machine vision, deep learning, autonomous weeding, targeted spraying, yield estimation, and robotic harvest systems, together with adoption efforts and performance limitations.
sources.title: Automation for Specialty Crops

sources.supports: Reports an average U.S. producer age of 58.1, 38% of producers age 65 or older, increasing multi-producer farms, and continuing producer aging, supporting a succession-pressure proxy.
sources.title: 2022 Census of Agriculture: Farm Producers

sources.supports: Reports record cultivated blueberry production of 789.5 million pounds in 2024, with 55% going to fresh markets, and describes different market destinations for cultivated and wild berries.
sources.title: Most cultivated blueberries are destined for the fresh market, while wild blueberries are bound for processing

sources.supports: Distinguishes marketing contracts, where farmers retain commodity ownership, from production contracts, where contractors generally own the commodity and compensate growers for services; reports production contracts at 2% of farms in 2020 and crop contracting concentrated in marketing contracts.
sources.title: Contracts are common in animal and crop production

sources.supports: Reports increasing import shares of U.S. fresh-fruit availability through 2023 and identifies blueberries and raspberries among crops with substantial import-share increases, supporting the domestic-demand competition caveat.
sources.title: U.S. fresh fruit and vegetable supplies continue to rely on imports

title: Berry (except Strawberry) Farming
